basic_info.incident_name: Chevron Richmond Refinery Pipe Rupture and Fire
basic_info.incident_name_cn: 雪佛龙里士满炼油厂管道破裂及火灾事故

process_context.operating_conditions: High-temperature (≈270°C), high-pressure service in crude distillation unit; cyclic thermal loading

failure_mechanism.root_cause: Chloride-induced stress corrosion cracking (CISCC) of carbon steel pipe due to inadequate inspection and corrosion management.
failure_mechanism.causal_chain: Inadequate mechanical integrity program → Unrecognized chloride contamination → Stress corrosion cracking initiation and growth → Loss of pipe wall thickness → Sudden rupture under operating pressure → Hydrocarbon release → Ignition → Fire
failure_mechanism.physical_principles: Electrochemical corrosion, residual stress, chloride ion diffusion, brittle fracture under tensile stress

protection_system.safeguard_failures: Inspection intervals too long; no targeted CISCC screening; thickness measurements missed cracked regions due to inaccessible geometry and lack of advanced NDE; PSM failed to identify chloride hazard in sidecut stream.
csb_recommendations: Revise API RP 571 to require CISCC risk assessment for all carbon steel piping exposed to chlorides
Mandate use of advanced NDE (e.g., phased array UT) for high-risk piping circuits
Require process hazard analyses (PHAs) to explicitly evaluate corrosion mechanisms beyond general 'corrosion' guide words

embedding_text: On August 6, 2012, a carbon steel 4-sidecut line in Chevron's Crude Unit #4 ruptured during operation, releasing hot hydrocarbons that ignited into a major fire. The failure resulted from undetected chloride-induced stress corrosion cracking—caused by chronic chloride contamination, insufficient corrosion monitoring, and absence of CISCC-specific safeguards. Operating at ~270°C with naphtha/gas oil, the pipe lost structural integrity without warning. This HAZOP-relevant 'No Integrity' deviation highlights critical gaps in mechanical integrity programs for aging refinery infrastructure.
